diagrams.title: Requirements Elicitation Process Flow
diagrams.description: A horizontal flowchart showing the sequential steps in requirements elicitation from project initiation to final documentation
elements: Project Start → Stakeholder Identification → Select Elicitation Techniques → Conduct Elicitation Activities → Document Requirements → Review and Validate → Requirements Specification
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the systematic, iterative nature of requirements elicitation and understand that it involves multiple feedback loops rather than a linear process

diagrams.title: Security Requirements Elicitation Framework
diagrams.description: A layered diagram showing security requirements elicitation integrated with threat analysis and risk assessment
elements: Bottom Layer: Business Context and Assets → Middle Layer: Threat Identification (Attack Vectors, Threat Agents) → Top Layer: Security Requirements (Authentication, Authorization, Data Protection, Input Validation) → Side Layer: Elicitation Techniques (Interviews, Workshops, Abuse Cases, Threat Modeling)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how security requirements are derived from understanding business assets, identifying threats, and applying specific elicitation techniques

diagrams.title: Elicitation Techniques Classification
diagrams.description: A tree diagram categorizing elicitation techniques into Direct, Indirect, and Modern categories with specific methods under each
elements: Direct Methods (Interviews, Surveys, Questionnaires) → Indirect Methods (Document Analysis, Observation, Protocol Analysis) → Modern Methods (Prototyping, JAD Sessions, Brainstorming, Use Case Development) → Agile Techniques (User Stories, Backlog Grooming, Sprint Planning)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the full spectrum of elicitation techniques and can select appropriate methods based on project context and stakeholder availability

diagrams.title: Stakeholder Analysis Matrix for Requirements
diagrams.description: A 2x2 matrix mapping stakeholders by their influence and interest in the project, with recommended elicitation strategies for each quadrant
diagrams.description2: Quadrants: High Influence/High Interest (Manage Closely - Joint Workshops), High Influence/Low Interest (Keep Satisfied - Regular Updates), Low Influence/High Interest (Keep Informed - Newsletters), Low Influence/Low Interest (Monitor - Minimal Contact)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to prioritize stakeholder engagement and select appropriate communication strategies based on stakeholder categorization

diagrams.title: From Elicitation to Use Case Development
diagrams.description: A vertical flow diagram showing the transformation from raw elicitation data to structured use cases
diagrams.description2: Shows: Raw Stakeholder Input → Requirements List → Use Case Identification → Use Case Specification (Actor, Preconditions, Flow of Events, Postconditions, Exceptions) → Use Case Diagram → Requirements Model
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how elicitation results are refined and structured into formal use case documentation

charts.title: Direct vs Indirect Elicitation Techniques
charts.content: Comparison showing: Direct Methods (Interviews - High stakeholder contact, immediate clarification; Surveys - Good for large groups, statistical analysis; Questionnaires - Quick, standardized responses) vs Indirect Methods (Document Analysis - Historical data, no stakeholder bias; Observation - Real behavior, time-intensive; Protocol Analysis - Think-aloud, cognitive insights) with columns for Advantages, Disadvantages, Best Use Cases, and Time/Cost Requirements

charts.title: Security Requirements Elicitation Process
charts.content: Start: Identify System Assets → Conduct Stakeholder Interviews for Security Concerns → Develop Abuse Cases (What can go wrong) → Perform Threat Modeling (STRIDE, DREAD) → Identify Security Requirements → Prioritize Requirements (Critical/High/Medium/Low) → Document in Security Requirements Specification → Link to Traceability Matrix → Validate with Stakeholders → End

charts.title: Security Requirements Categories
charts.content: Four-column classification: Authentication Requirements (Password policies, MFA, Session management, Account lockout), Authorization Requirements (Role-based access, Permission levels, Privilege escalation prevention), Data Protection Requirements (Encryption at rest/transit, Data masking, Secure storage, Privacy compliance), Input Validation Requirements (Input sanitization, Parameterized queries, File upload validation, Boundary checking)

charts.title: Traditional vs Agile Requirements Elicitation
charts.content: Traditional: Heavy upfront documentation, Sequential process, Formal interviews/workshops, Extensive requirements specs → Agile: Continuous elicitation, Iterative refinement, User stories through collaboration, Product backlog with priorities, Sprint-based validation, Changes welcome throughout the cycle

branches: Purpose: Discover true stakeholder needs through systematic engagement
Techniques: Interview, Survey, Observation, Prototyping, JAD, Document Analysis
Inputs: Business goals, Existing systems, Stakeholder list, Project charter
Outputs: Requirements document, Use cases, User stories, Acceptance criteria
Challenges: Stakeholder communication, Scope creep, Ambiguous requirements, Changing needs
Security Focus: Abuse cases, Threat modeling, Security use cases, Risk assessment
Tools: Questionnaires, Prototyping tools, Collaboration platforms, Requirements management software
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting elicitation purposes, techniques, inputs/outputs, challenges, and security-specific considerations - helps students see the holistic view of requirements elicitation in software engineering
